news_summary: Recent scientific advances reveal key insights into aging-related belly fat, essential genes in human development, shared laughter rhythms with great apes, and effective approaches to overcoming phone addiction.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées scientifiques apportent des éclairages essentiels sur la graisse abdominale liée au vieillissement, les gènes essentiels au développement humain, les rythmes de rire communs avec les grands singes, ainsi que les méthodes efficaces pour surmonter la dépendance au téléphone.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances científicos aportan conocimientos clave sobre la grasa abdominal relacionada con el envejecimiento, los genes esenciales para el desarrollo humano, los ritmos de risa que compartimos con los grandes simios y los métodos eficaces para superar la adicción al móvil.

articles.title: Scientists discover what triggers belly fat as we age
articles.summary: Aging may trigger the appearance of specialized stem cells that supercharge the body's ability to create new belly fat. The discovery reveals a potential biological driver of middle-age weight gain and a promising target for future anti-obesity treatments.
articles.content:
Many people notice a familiar change as they get older: the waistline gradually expands, even when overall body weight does not change dramatically. This increase in abdominal fat is more than a cosmetic concern. Excess belly fat has been linked to slower metabolism, accelerated aging, type 2 diabetes, heart disease, and other chronic health problems.

Scientists have long known that body composition changes with age, but exactly why fat tends to accumulate around the midsection has remained unclear.

Now, researchers at City of Hope have identified what may be a key biological driver of age-related belly fat. Their findings, published in the journal Science, point to a newly identified type of stem cell that appears during aging and may help fuel the production of new fat cells. The discovery could eventually lead to new strategies for reducing abdominal fat and promoting healthier aging.

"People often lose muscle and gain body fat as they age -- even when their body weight remains the same," said Qiong (Annabel) Wang, Ph.D., the study's co-corresponding author and an associate professor of molecular and cellular endocrinology at City of Hope's Arthur Riggs Diabetes & Metabolism Research Institute, a leading center for diabetes research. "We discovered aging triggers the arrival of a new type of adult stem cell and enhances the body's massive production of new fat cells, especially around the belly."

Looking Beyond Enlarged Fat Cells

The research team worked with scientists at UCLA and conducted a series of experiments in mice that were later supported by studies of human cells.

Their investigation focused on white adipose tissue (WAT), the body's primary fat-storage tissue. White adipose tissue is responsible for storing excess energy and is a major contributor to weight gain and belly fat accumulation.

Scientists have long known that existing fat cells can become larger as people age. However, the researchers suspected that another process might also be contributing to expanding waistlines: the creation of entirely new fat cells.

If true, that would mean aging fat tissue could continue growing not just by enlarging existing cells, but by constantly adding new ones.

To test this idea, the team studied adipocyte progenitor cells (APCs), a type of stem cell found within fat tissue. These cells serve as precursors that can mature into fully developed fat cells.

Older Stem Cells Produced Far More Fat

The researchers transplanted APCs from both young and older mice into a separate group of young mice.

The results were striking. APCs taken from older animals generated large numbers of new fat cells.

The opposite experiment produced a very different outcome. When APCs from young mice were transplanted into older mice, they generated relatively few new fat cells.

This suggested that the ability to aggressively produce fat was built into the older APCs themselves and did not depend on the age of the animal receiving them.

To understand what was happening at a molecular level, the researchers used single-cell RNA sequencing, a technique that allows scientists to examine gene activity in individual cells.

The analysis revealed that APCs were relatively quiet in young mice. In middle-aged mice, however, these cells became highly active and began producing large numbers of new fat cells.

"While most adult stem cells' capacity to grow wanes with age, the opposite holds true with APCs -- aging unlocks these cells' power to evolve and spread," said Adolfo Garcia-Ocana, Ph.D., the Ruth B. & Robert K. Lanman Endowed Chair in Gene Regulation & Drug Discovery Research and chair of the Department of Molecular & Cellular Endocrinology at City of Hope. "This is the first evidence that our bellies expand with age due to the APCs' high output of new fat cells."

Discovery of a New Age-Related Stem Cell

The scientists found that aging did more than simply activate APCs.

As mice reached middle age, some APCs transformed into a newly identified stem cell population called committed preadipocytes, age-specific (CP-As).

These cells appeared specifically during aging and proved especially effective at producing new fat cells. Their emergence may help explain why older mice gained more fat as they aged.

The researchers then searched for the biological signals controlling this process.

They identified an important signaling pathway known as leukemia inhibitory factor receptor (LIFR). Signaling pathways are communication systems that allow cells to receive instructions and coordinate their behavior. In this case, LIFR appeared to play a major role in helping CP-A cells multiply and develop into fat cells.

"We discovered that the body's fat-making process is driven by LIFR. While young mice don't require this signal to make fat, older mice do," explained Wang. "Our research indicates that LIFR plays a crucial role in triggering CP-As to create new fat cells and expand belly fat in older mice."

Similar Fat-Producing Cells Found in Humans

To determine whether the findings might apply beyond mice, the team analyzed human tissue samples from people of different ages using the same single-cell RNA sequencing approach.

The researchers identified cells that closely resembled the newly discovered CP-As. These cells were found in greater numbers in tissue from middle-aged individuals.

The human CP-As also showed a strong ability to generate new fat cells, suggesting that a similar biological process may occur in people.

"Our findings highlight the importance of controlling new fat-cell formation to address age-related obesity," said Wang. "Understanding the role of CP-As in metabolic disorders and how these cells emerge during aging could lead to new medical solutions for reducing belly fat and improving health and longevity."

A Potential New Target for Age-Related Obesity

Although more research is needed, the discovery provides scientists with a promising new target for future therapies.

Researchers now plan to track CP-A cells in animal studies, investigate how these cells behave in humans, and explore ways to block or eliminate them. If successful, such approaches could potentially help prevent the accumulation of belly fat that commonly accompanies aging.

The study's first authors were City of Hope researcher Guan Wang, Ph.D., and UCLA researcher Gaoyan Li, Ph.D.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery identifying a biological driver of age-related belly fat accumulation, which has broad health implications. The finding of a new type of stem cell linked to fat production during aging offers a promising target for future anti-obesity treatments that could improve metabolic health and longevity for many people. The research is well-detailed, involving both animal and human studies, and focuses on a single impactful topic.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find stem cells that cause belly fat to grow as we age
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people notice their belly gets bigger as they get older, even if their overall weight stays the same. This belly fat is linked to health problems like diabetes and heart disease. Scientists have wanted to understand why this happens.

**What happened** – Researchers at City of Hope discovered a special type of stem cell that appears with aging and helps the body make more belly fat. They studied mice and human tissue and found these cells, called committed preadipocytes age-specific (CP-As), are more active in middle-aged individuals. These cells create new fat cells, making belly fat grow.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows belly fat grows not just by fat cells getting bigger, but by new fat cells being made. The study also found a key signal called LIFR that helps these special stem cells multiply. Finding these cells and signals means scientists now have new targets to develop treatments that could reduce belly fat in older people.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue studying CP-A cells to understand how they work in humans and look for ways to block or remove them. This could lead to new medicines that stop belly fat from growing as people age, helping improve health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found special stem cells that cause belly fat to grow with age, opening the door to new treatments for age-related weight gain.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des cellules souches qui font grossir le ventre avec l’âge
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes remarquent que leur ventre grossit en vieillissant, même si leur poids ne change pas beaucoup. Cette graisse du ventre est liée à des problèmes de santé comme le diabète et les maladies cardiaques. Les scientifiques voulaient comprendre pourquoi cela arrive.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de City of Hope ont découvert un type spécial de cellules souches qui apparaissent avec l’âge et aident le corps à fabriquer plus de graisse du ventre. Ils ont étudié des souris et des tissus humains et ont trouvé ces cellules, appelées préadipocytes engagés spécifiques à l’âge (CP-As), plus actives chez les personnes d’âge moyen. Ces cellules créent de nouvelles cellules graisseuses, faisant grossir le ventre.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que la graisse du ventre grossit non seulement parce que les cellules graisseuses s’agrandissent, mais aussi parce que de nouvelles cellules sont créées. L’étude a aussi trouvé un signal clé appelé LIFR qui aide ces cellules souches spéciales à se multiplier. Trouver ces cellules et signaux offre de nouvelles cibles pour développer des traitements afin de réduire la graisse du ventre chez les personnes âgées.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier les cellules CP-A pour comprendre leur fonctionnement chez l’humain et chercher des moyens de les bloquer ou de les éliminer. Cela pourrait mener à de nouveaux médicaments pour empêcher la croissance de la graisse du ventre avec l’âge et améliorer la santé.

**Message en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont découvert des cellules souches spéciales qui font grossir la graisse du ventre avec l’âge, ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux traitements contre la prise de poids liée à l’âge.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren células madre que hacen crecer la grasa del vientre con la edad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas notan que su vientre se agranda al envejecer, aunque su peso total no cambie mucho. Esta grasa abdominal está relacionada con problemas de salud como diabetes y enfermedades del corazón. Los científicos querían saber por qué sucede esto.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de City of Hope descubrieron un tipo especial de células madre que aparecen con la edad y ayudan al cuerpo a crear más grasa en el vientre. Estudiaron ratones y tejidos humanos y encontraron estas células, llamadas preadipocitos comprometidos específicos de la edad (CP-As), que están más activas en personas de mediana edad. Estas células crean nuevas células de grasa, haciendo que el vientre crezca.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que la grasa del vientre crece no solo porque las células grasas se hacen más grandes, sino porque se crean nuevas células. El estudio también encontró una señal clave llamada LIFR que ayuda a estas células madre especiales a multiplicarse. Encontrar estas células y señales da nuevas metas para desarrollar tratamientos que puedan reducir la grasa abdominal en personas mayores.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando las células CP-A para entender cómo funcionan en humanos y buscar formas de bloquearlas o eliminarlas. Esto podría llevar a nuevos medicamentos para detener el aumento de grasa en el vientre con la edad y mejorar la salud.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han encontrado células madre especiales que causan que la grasa del vientre crezca con la edad, lo que abre la puerta a nuevos tratamientos para el aumento de peso relacionado con la edad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, softly colored painting of a gentle garden scene where small, glowing seed-like stem cells sprout and multiply into clusters of round, warm-toned fat cells arranged like blossoming flowers around a central, glowing waist-shaped vine, symbolizing the growth of new belly fat driven by aging stem cells and their signaling pathways.

articles.title: Base editing reveals an essential role for NANOG in human embryogenesis
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10792-1Base editing reveals an essential role for NANOG in human embryogenesis
articles.content: Understanding how the first cell lineages in human development are specified and maintained has fundamental importance and clinical implications for regenerative medicine, infertility and pregnancy loss. While mouse models have provided valuable insights into transcription factors regulating early development, translating these findings to human embryos has been limited by ethical, technical and biological constraints. Functional studies of transcription factors in human embryos have been hindered by nuclease-based genome-editing approaches that induce genotoxicity1-3. To overcome this, we applied adenine base editing (ABE8e)4,5 to precisely target an exon splice donor site, resulting in a splicing defect and functional knockout of NANOG, representing the first application of base editing to study a developmental regulator in human embryos. This approach did not trigger genotoxicity and showed limited off-target editing. Loss of NANOG disrupts pluripotent epiblast specification and instead cells differentiate toward a primitive endoderm (yolk sac) or trophectoderm (placental) transcriptional programme. Retention of primitive endoderm differentiation in NANOG-edited human embryos reveals a functional compensation distinct from mouse, underscoring the importance of directly investigating human development. Our findings demonstrate an essential role for NANOG in human pluripotency and epiblast specification, and highlight the utility of base editing for functional interrogation of human development.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough using precise base editing to study human embryogenesis, revealing the essential role of NANOG in early human development. This has broad implications for regenerative medicine, infertility, and pregnancy loss, benefiting many people. The study overcomes previous technical and ethical limitations and provides important insights distinct from animal models, indicating substantial real-world impact and scientific advancement.
articles.personality_title: New gene editing technique reveals key role of NANOG in early human development
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists want to understand how the first cells in a human embryo develop into different types. This knowledge is important for treating infertility, pregnancy problems, and for future medical treatments using stem cells. However, studying human embryos is difficult because of ethical rules and limits with older gene-editing methods.

**What happened** – Researchers used a new, precise gene-editing tool called adenine base editing (ABE8e) to study the NANOG gene in human embryos. NANOG is a gene thought to be important for early development. By editing this gene without causing harmful side effects, they found that when NANOG doesn’t work, embryos cannot form the main group of cells that become the body (called the epiblast). Instead, cells turn into other types that support the embryo, like those forming the yolk sac or placenta.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that NANOG is essential for creating the cells that will grow into a human body. It also reveals differences between human and mouse development, meaning we cannot always rely on animal studies to understand humans. The new editing method is safer and more accurate, opening doors for future research on human development without damaging the embryo.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely use this new gene-editing tool to study other important genes in human embryos. This could help explain causes of infertility or pregnancy loss and improve treatments. It also allows researchers to explore human development in ways not possible before.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using a precise gene-editing method, scientists showed that the NANOG gene is crucial for early human embryo cells to develop properly, offering new insights into human development and potential medical advances.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle technique d’édition génétique révèle le rôle clé de NANOG dans le développement embryonnaire humain
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre comment les premières cellules d’un embryon humain se développent en différents types. Cette connaissance est importante pour traiter l’infertilité, les problèmes de grossesse et pour de futurs traitements médicaux utilisant les cellules souches. Étudier les embryons humains est difficile à cause des règles éthiques et des limites des anciennes méthodes d’édition génétique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont utilisé un nouvel outil précis d’édition génétique appelé édition de base d’adénine (ABE8e) pour étudier le gène NANOG dans des embryons humains. NANOG est un gène important pour le développement précoce. En modifiant ce gène sans effets secondaires nocifs, ils ont découvert que sans NANOG, les embryons ne peuvent pas former le groupe principal de cellules qui deviendront le corps (appelé épiblaste). À la place, les cellules deviennent d’autres types qui soutiennent l’embryon, comme celles du sac vitellin ou du placenta.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que NANOG est essentiel pour créer les cellules qui formeront un corps humain. Elle révèle aussi des différences entre le développement humain et celui de la souris, ce qui signifie que les études animales ne suffisent pas toujours. La nouvelle méthode est plus sûre et précise, ouvrant la voie à de futures recherches sans endommager l’embryon.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement utiliser cet outil pour étudier d’autres gènes importants dans les embryons humains. Cela pourrait aider à comprendre les causes d’infertilité ou de fausses couches et améliorer les traitements. Cela permet aussi d’explorer le développement humain comme jamais auparavant.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Grâce à une méthode d’édition génétique précise, les scientifiques ont montré que le gène NANOG est crucial pour que les cellules de l’embryon humain se développent correctement, offrant de nouvelles connaissances sur le développement humain et des avancées médicales possibles.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva técnica de edición genética revela el papel clave de NANOG en el desarrollo embrionario humano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos quieren entender cómo las primeras células de un embrión humano se desarrollan en diferentes tipos. Esta información es importante para tratar la infertilidad, problemas del embarazo y para futuros tratamientos médicos con células madre. Estudiar embriones humanos es difícil debido a normas éticas y limitaciones de métodos antiguos de edición genética.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores usaron una nueva herramienta precisa llamada edición de base de adenina (ABE8e) para estudiar el gen NANOG en embriones humanos. NANOG es un gen importante para el desarrollo temprano. Al editar este gen sin causar daños, descubrieron que sin NANOG, los embriones no pueden formar el grupo principal de células que se convertirán en el cuerpo (llamado epiblasto). En cambio, las células se convierten en otros tipos que apoyan al embrión, como las del saco vitelino o la placenta.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que NANOG es esencial para crear las células que formarán un cuerpo humano. También revela diferencias entre el desarrollo humano y el de ratones, por lo que no siempre se puede confiar en estudios con animales. El nuevo método es más seguro y preciso, permitiendo futuras investigaciones sin dañar el embrión.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos probablemente usarán esta nueva herramienta para estudiar otros genes importantes en embriones humanos. Esto podría ayudar a entender causas de infertilidad o pérdida del embarazo y mejorar tratamientos. También permite explorar el desarrollo humano de formas antes imposibles.

**Frase clave** – Usando un método preciso de edición genética, los científicos demostraron que el gen NANOG es crucial para que las células del embrión humano se desarrollen correctamente, ofreciendo nuevas ideas sobre el desarrollo humano y posibles avances médicos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a delicate, glowing embryonic structure composed of intertwined, translucent cellular spheres gently illuminated from within, surrounded by softly shifting layers symbolizing the epiblast, primitive endoderm, and trophectoderm lineages, with a subtle golden thread weaving through the spheres representing the precise, gentle influence of base editing on the essential NANOG factor, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones.

articles.title: Daily briefing: Humans and great apes giggle in the same rhythms
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02060-zThe findings hint that vocal motor control might have evolved from a shared ancestor. Plus, sea turtles’ navigation skills are only so-so and what’s next for the International Space Station.
articles.content:
The findings hint that vocal motor control might have evolved from a shared ancestor. Plus, sea turtles’ navigation skills are only so-so and what’s next for the International Space Station.

Hello Nature readers, would you like to get this Briefing in your inbox free every day? Sign up here.

When laughing, chimpanzees and other great apes produce similar patterns of vocalization to human children.Credit: Jean-Philippe Ksiazek/AFP via Getty

Chimpanzees (Pan troglodytes), gorillas (Gorilla gorilla) and children laugh in similar rhythms when tickled. Researchers found that kids and apes left evenly spaced intervals between laughing sounds during a tickle attack, though children had a faster laughter rhythm compared with apes. Laughter might have picked up pace during the course of human evolution, the team suggests, which could reveal “something about laughter itself, but also, in a way, about the evolution of human speech”, says primatologist and study co-author Chiara De Gregorio.

Nature | 4 min read

Reference: Communications Biology paper

A newly discovered gene ‘megacluster’ in Streptomyces bacteria enables them to produce a variety of potent antibiotic compounds. These compounds act as a multi-pronged offensive weapon against other species, with each targeting different stages of the bacterial metabolic process. It’s more difficult for bacteria to develop resistance to attacks that hit several targets, so the discovery could lead to the development of new antibiotics, experts say. The research has “discovered something new in a system so extensively studied — hidden in plain sight,” says medicinal chemist Mark Blaskovich.

Nature | 4 min read

Reference: Nature paper

Green sea turtles (Chelonia mydas) use Earth’s geomagnetic field as a map, but are only able to follow it approximately. Researchers tracked eight turtles as they made a journey of more than 1000 kilometres. They found that the animals tended to plough ahead in one direction for extended periods, then stop to reorient when they’d strayed from their course. The turtles have only “an approximate idea of where they are and where they’re going”, says marine ecologist and study co-author Graeme Hays. So they take “circuitous routes that eventually get them where they want to be”.

Science | 5 min read

Reference: Science Advances paper

Features & opinion

Scientists are exploring the potential of printable inks that could prevent scarring after burn trauma. These gels are laced with cultures of skin cells to coax the body into rebuilding healthy skin after burns, rather than launching the frenzied emergency-repair pathway that leads to scar tissue. In initial clinical trials, the inks will have to be administered by doctors with a syringe. But researchers are also developing robotic arms that could one day precisely map a person’s injury and print the gel directly onto it.

Knowable | 7 min read

Neutrinos, a type of fundamental subatomic particle, are elusive. They carry no charge, have almost no mass and generally pass through objects and living things with no discernible effect — all factors that make them difficult to detect. Collecting data on these mysterious particles requires some of the most ambitious experimental set-ups in the business, which are detecting neutrinos in Antarctic ice, the caves of an active Canadian mine and at the bottom of the Mediterranean sea.

Quanta | Picture-filled scroll

Earlier this month, astronauts on board the International Space Station (ISS) had to shelter in a docked capsule because of an air leak in the Russian part of the station. All was well in the end, but it was “a dose of engineering reality”, writes Christopher Newman, an expert in space law and policy. “NASA and Russia’s space agency Roscosmos dispute the seriousness of the problem,” he writes. “Not only is the structure under strain, but also the fabric of the agreement that keeps the ISS running.” Private space stations were always envisaged as the next step, but they’re ill-prepared to take over, Newman argues.

The Conversation | 8 min read

Image of the week

This newly described ‘ballista’ spider (Propostira sp.) uses a spring-loaded snare to catch prey. The arachnid’s trap flings unsuspecting green tree ants (Oecophylla smaragdina) into a waiting web at an acceleration of more than 1,300 metres per second squared, around 100 times greater than that of a Formula One car, says sensory biologist and study co-author Ajay Narendra. The team aren’t sure why these spiders evolved this strategy, but suggest that it could protect them from angry worker ants that might attempt to rescue their colleague. (CNN | 6 min read)

Reference: Current Biology paper (Ajay Narendra et al./Current Biology (CC BY 4.0))
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant scientific discovery about laughter rhythms shared between humans and great apes, providing insights into the evolution of human speech and social behavior. Additionally, it mentions promising developments in printable inks for burn trauma treatment, which could improve healing and reduce scarring, representing a meaningful advancement in medical technology with broad potential benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find humans and great apes laugh in similar rhythms
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied how humans and other animals communicate and express emotions. Laughter is one way people share joy and connect with others. Understanding its roots can tell us about how humans evolved to speak and interact.

**What happened** – Researchers discovered that chimpanzees, gorillas, and human children laugh in similar rhythmic patterns when tickled. While children laugh faster, both apes and kids leave evenly spaced pauses between laughs. This study, published in Communications Biology in June 2026, suggests that the ability to control vocal sounds during laughter may come from a shared ancestor.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it links laughter to the evolution of human speech. It shows that some aspects of how we produce sounds and communicate might have started millions of years ago in common ancestors with great apes. Understanding these rhythms could help scientists learn more about how complex speech developed.

**What's next step** – Future research may explore how laughter rhythms relate to other vocal sounds in humans and animals. Scientists might also study how laughter affects social bonding and communication in different species, giving more clues about human language origins.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Humans and great apes share similar laughter rhythms, revealing a possible evolutionary connection to how human speech developed.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les humains et les grands singes rient avec des rythmes similaires
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps comment les humains et les animaux communiquent et expriment leurs émotions. Le rire est une façon pour les gens de partager la joie et de créer des liens. Comprendre ses origines peut expliquer comment les humains ont évolué pour parler et interagir.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert que les chimpanzés, les gorilles et les enfants humains rient selon des rythmes similaires lorsqu’ils sont chatouillés. Bien que les enfants rient plus vite, les deux laissent des pauses régulières entre les rires. Cette étude, publiée en juin 2026 dans Communications Biology, suggère que la capacité à contrôler les sons de la voix pendant le rire vient d’un ancêtre commun.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle relie le rire à l’évolution de la parole humaine. Elle montre que certains aspects de la façon dont nous produisons des sons et communiquons ont pu commencer il y a des millions d’années chez des ancêtres communs avec les grands singes. Comprendre ces rythmes peut aider les scientifiques à mieux connaître le développement du langage complexe.

**Prochaine étape** – Les recherches futures pourraient explorer comment les rythmes du rire sont liés à d’autres sons vocaux chez les humains et les animaux. Les scientifiques pourraient aussi étudier comment le rire influence les liens sociaux et la communication dans différentes espèces, apportant plus d’indices sur l’origine du langage humain.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les humains et les grands singes partagent des rythmes de rire similaires, révélant un lien évolutif possible avec le développement de la parole humaine.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren que humanos y grandes simios ríen con ritmos similares
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo cómo los humanos y otros animales se comunican y expresan emociones. La risa es una forma en que las personas comparten alegría y se conectan. Entender sus orígenes puede contar cómo los humanos evolucionaron para hablar e interactuar.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores descubrieron que chimpancés, gorilas y niños humanos ríen con patrones rítmicos similares cuando les hacen cosquillas. Aunque los niños ríen más rápido, ambos dejan pausas regulares entre las risas. Este estudio, publicado en Communications Biology en junio de 2026, sugiere que la capacidad de controlar sonidos vocales durante la risa puede venir de un ancestro común.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque conecta la risa con la evolución del habla humana. Muestra que algunos aspectos de cómo producimos sonidos y comunicamos podrían haber empezado hace millones de años en ancestros comunes con grandes simios. Entender estos ritmos puede ayudar a los científicos a aprender más sobre cómo se desarrolló el habla compleja.

**Próximo paso** – Investigaciones futuras podrían explorar cómo los ritmos de la risa se relacionan con otros sonidos vocales en humanos y animales. Los científicos también podrían estudiar cómo la risa afecta la unión social y la comunicación en diferentes especies, dando más pistas sobre el origen del lenguaje humano.

**Frase clave** – Humanos y grandes simios comparten ritmos similares de risa, revelando una posible conexión evolutiva con el desarrollo del habla humana.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a joyful young child and a chimpanzee sitting side by side, both surrounded by gentle, rhythmic sound waves visually echoing their laughter patterns in soft, natural earth tones against a simple, abstract background.

articles.title: I'm in therapy for my 14-hour-a-day phone addiction and I'm determined to beat it
articles.summary: Addiction treatment centres say more clients are coming for help with "out-of-control" phone use.
articles.content:
In 2017, several people concerned about their tech and internet use banded together to create Internet and Technology Addicts Anonymous (ITAA), a global fellowship inspired by Alcoholics Anonymous (AA).

Jenny is one of their members. At the height of her phone addiction, she would not sleep for days. She would barely eat or drink, her dependency was so strong.

"I would lose chunks of my life," explains the 30-year-old, who doesn't want the BBC to use her real name.

She didn't care what popped up on her screen - a film, a series, a short video - as long as she was watching something.

"I did not realise how I addicted I was until I was in withdrawal and I had to ask friends and family to keep my devices under lock and key," Jenny recalls.

"It was so bad, I thought I am going to die if I don't watch something."

If she relapsed, she would resort to taking or "borrowing without permission" a laptop or a smartphone from her family.

But then the guilt and shame would kick in, and she would want to stream more content to block out the feelings.

After years of "searching for help", she came across ITAA and followed their 12 steps. She is now in recovery and has not streamed or watched anything for five years.

Jenny says she feels comfortable with having a basic phone, and going online for her job. "I'm now in control," she says.

Another ITAA member, Tom, says his addiction led him to dark places. He could lose whole months of his life to his phone and other screens.

"I would binge for 10 hours straight – I could be listening to music, watching something on YouTube, scrolling through social media and playing a video game – all at the same time.

"Then I would go for a two-hour walk, and binge again. This could go on for months."

Tom's addiction was so overpowering that it led to him losing his business and his sense of purpose in life.

"I became suicidal," he says.

"I am starting to get real joy in life again. I play lots of pickle ball, I get outside and I go to the gym."
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant health issue—phone addiction—and presents a positive story of recovery through a support group modeled on AA. It shows real-world impact by addressing a growing mental health concern affecting many people globally, with detailed personal stories and evidence of meaningful improvement in individuals' lives.
articles.personality_title: Support group helps people overcome phone addiction and regain control
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Phone addiction is a growing problem where people spend many hours a day using their devices, often losing track of time and neglecting their health and responsibilities. In 2017, a group called Internet and Technology Addicts Anonymous (ITAA) was created to help people struggling with this issue, inspired by Alcoholics Anonymous.

**What happened** – Jenny and Tom are two members of ITAA who shared their stories. Jenny used to spend so much time on her phone that she would go days without sleeping or eating. She felt trapped by her addiction until she joined ITAA and followed their 12-step program. Now, she has not watched or streamed anything for five years and uses a simple phone for her work. Tom suffered similarly, spending up to 10 hours a day on his phone and other screens, which affected his business and mental health. After joining ITAA, he found new joy in activities like pickleball and going to the gym.

**Impact** – These stories show that phone addiction can seriously harm people’s lives, but recovery is possible with the right support. ITAA provides a space for people to share their experiences and follow a structured program to regain control. Jenny and Tom’s improvements prove that change can happen, even after long struggles.

**What's next step** – As more people recognize phone addiction as a real health problem, groups like ITAA may grow and reach more individuals in need. Their success stories could inspire others to seek help and encourage health professionals to offer more treatment options.

**One-sentence takeaway** – With support from groups like ITAA, people struggling with phone addiction can recover and rebuild healthier, happier lives.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un groupe de soutien aide à surmonter l’addiction au téléphone et à reprendre le contrôle
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’addiction au téléphone est un problème croissant où les gens passent de nombreuses heures par jour sur leurs appareils, perdant souvent la notion du temps et négligeant leur santé et leurs responsabilités. En 2017, un groupe appelé Internet and Technology Addicts Anonymous (ITAA) a été créé pour aider les personnes concernées, inspiré des Alcooliques Anonymes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Jenny et Tom sont deux membres d’ITAA qui ont partagé leurs histoires. Jenny passait tellement de temps sur son téléphone qu’elle pouvait rester des jours sans dormir ni manger. Elle se sentait prisonnière de son addiction jusqu’à ce qu’elle rejoigne ITAA et suive leur programme en 12 étapes. Aujourd’hui, elle ne regarde plus rien depuis cinq ans et utilise un téléphone simple pour son travail. Tom vivait une situation similaire, passant jusqu’à 10 heures par jour sur son téléphone et d’autres écrans, ce qui a affecté son entreprise et sa santé mentale. Après avoir rejoint ITAA, il a retrouvé de la joie grâce à des activités comme le pickleball et la salle de sport.

**Impact** – Ces histoires montrent que l’addiction au téléphone peut sérieusement nuire à la vie des gens, mais que la guérison est possible avec un bon soutien. ITAA offre un espace pour partager ses expériences et suivre un programme structuré pour reprendre le contrôle. Les progrès de Jenny et Tom prouvent que le changement est possible, même après de longues difficultés.

**Prochaine étape** – À mesure que de plus en plus de personnes reconnaissent l’addiction au téléphone comme un vrai problème de santé, des groupes comme ITAA pourraient grandir et aider davantage de personnes. Leurs succès pourraient encourager d’autres à demander de l’aide et pousser les professionnels de santé à offrir plus d’options de traitement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Avec le soutien d’ITAA, les personnes souffrant d’addiction au téléphone peuvent se rétablir et retrouver une vie plus saine et épanouie.
articles.personality_title_es: Grupo de apoyo ayuda a superar la adicción al teléfono y recuperar el control
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La adicción al teléfono es un problema creciente donde las personas pasan muchas horas al día usando sus dispositivos, perdiendo la noción del tiempo y descuidando su salud y responsabilidades. En 2017, se creó un grupo llamado Internet and Technology Addicts Anonymous (ITAA) para ayudar a quienes enfrentan este problema, inspirado en Alcohólicos Anónimos.

**Qué pasó** – Jenny y Tom son dos miembros de ITAA que compartieron sus historias. Jenny usaba tanto su teléfono que podía pasar días sin dormir ni comer. Se sentía atrapada por su adicción hasta que se unió a ITAA y siguió su programa de 12 pasos. Ahora, lleva cinco años sin ver ni transmitir nada y usa un teléfono básico para su trabajo. Tom vivió algo similar, pasando hasta 10 horas al día en su teléfono y otras pantallas, lo que afectó su negocio y salud mental. Después de unirse a ITAA, encontró alegría en actividades como el pickleball y el gimnasio.

**Impacto** – Estas historias muestran que la adicción al teléfono puede dañar mucho la vida de las personas, pero la recuperación es posible con el apoyo adecuado. ITAA ofrece un espacio para compartir experiencias y seguir un programa estructurado para recuperar el control. Las mejoras de Jenny y Tom demuestran que el cambio puede ocurrir, incluso después de largos problemas.

**Próximo paso** – A medida que más personas reconocen la adicción al teléfono como un problema real de salud, grupos como ITAA podrían crecer y ayudar a más personas. Sus historias de éxito pueden inspirar a otros a buscar ayuda y motivar a profesionales de la salud a ofrecer más opciones de tratamiento.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Con el apoyo de grupos como ITAA, quienes luchan contra la adicción al teléfono pueden recuperarse y construir vidas más saludables y felices.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two intertwined old-fashioned clocks melting gently into a calm river, their faces softly glowing like phone screens, surrounded by simple, muted earth tones and subtle silhouettes of locked boxes and broken chains resting nearby, symbolizing freedom from technology addiction and the reclaiming of time and life.
